description: Rows, pull-ups, pulldowns, pullovers, back isolation

exercises.name: Barbell Bent-Over Row
exercises.beginner_tips: Hinge forward to about 45 degrees, flat back, arms hanging. Pull bar to belly button by driving elbows back and squeezing shoulder blades. Lower slowly. DON'T jerk or round your back. If you can't hold the hinged position without lower back fatigue, switch to chest-supported rows.
exercises.notes: Major back builder. Grip width matters more than over/underhand for targeting: wider grip with elbows flared hits mid-traps and rhomboids, narrower grip with elbows tucked emphasizes lats. Underhand grip increases bicep involvement and allows heavier weight for some people. Torso angle also matters — more upright = more traps, more bent over = more lats.

exercises.name: Pendlay Row
exercises.beginner_tips: Torso parallel to the floor (much more horizontal than a regular bent-over row). Bar starts on the floor EVERY rep. Explosively row to your lower chest, then lower the bar back to the floor and reset. Each rep starts from a dead stop — no bouncing or momentum.
exercises.notes: Named after weightlifting coach Glenn Pendlay. The dead stop eliminates the stretch reflex, making every rep harder and stricter than a standard bent-over row. The parallel torso position maximizes lat recruitment. Excellent for building explosive pulling power. Requires good hip hinge mechanics — master the bent-over row first.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Row (Single-Arm)
exercises.beginner_tips: One knee and hand on bench. Dumbbell in other hand, let it hang. Pull to hip by driving elbow back, squeeze shoulder blade. Lower slowly. Think about pulling your elbow toward the ceiling, not curling the weight with your arm.
exercises.notes: Best row for beginners — bench supports lower back. Great for fixing left/right imbalances since each side works independently. Can go quite heavy with some controlled body english for an overload effect (sometimes called a Kroc row).

exercises.name: Chest-Supported Dumbbell Row
exercises.beginner_tips: Bench at 30-45 degrees, lie face down with chest on the pad. Row both dumbbells by driving elbows back and squeezing shoulder blades. Your chest on the bench means ZERO lower back involvement — all the work goes to your back muscles.
exercises.notes: If bent-over rows hurt your lower back, use these instead. 100% back focus with zero cheating. The bench angle affects which back muscles are emphasized: steeper angle = more mid-traps/rhomboids, flatter angle = more lats.

exercises.name: Seal Row (Chest-Supported Barbell Row)
exercises.beginner_tips: Lie face down on a bench that's elevated enough for your arms to hang straight with the barbell (stack the bench on boxes or plates, or use a dedicated seal row station). Row the barbell to the underside of the bench by squeezing shoulder blades. Lower with control. Zero momentum possible.
exercises.notes: The ultimate anti-cheat row. Your entire body is supported so the ONLY way to move the weight is with your back muscles. Allows heavier loading than dumbbell chest-supported rows. Setup can be awkward if your gym doesn't have a dedicated station — stacking the bench on blocks works but takes time. Growing in popularity for good reason.

exercises.name: Seated Cable Row
exercises.beginner_tips: Sit with feet on platform, knees slightly bent. Pull handle to stomach, squeeze shoulder blades together and hold for a beat. Keep back straight — don't lean way back to cheat the weight. A slight forward lean at the start and slight backward lean at the finish is fine, but the movement should come from your arms and back, not your torso swinging.
exercises.notes: Constant cable tension throughout the movement. Different attachments change the emphasis: V-handle (neutral grip) = more lat emphasis with elbows tucked, wide bar = more mid-traps/rhomboids with elbows flared. The cable's constant tension profile makes this a great complement to barbell rows where the resistance curve is different.

exercises.name: T-Bar Row
exercises.beginner_tips: Straddle the bar, use the handles or a V-handle placed under the bar. Row to chest, squeezing shoulder blades. Keep back flat — don't round. The neutral grip feels natural for most people and is easy on the wrists.
exercises.notes: Neutral grip allows most people to row heavier than with a barbell. Classic bodybuilding exercise. The fixed end of the barbell creates a natural arc that many find more comfortable than straight barbell rows. If your gym doesn't have a T-bar station, wedge a barbell in a corner and use a V-handle.

exercises.name: Machine Row (Plate-Loaded or Cable)
exercises.beginner_tips: Chest against pad, grab handles, pull toward you squeezing shoulder blades together. The chest pad removes all lower back strain. Squeeze hard at the back position and control the return — don't just let the weight slam back.
exercises.notes: Very beginner-friendly. Chest pad supports you so you can focus entirely on pulling. Plate-loaded versions (like Hammer Strength) allow independent arm movement on some models, which helps identify and fix left/right imbalances.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Row
exercises.beginner_tips: Stand in front of the Smith machine, hinge forward with flat back, grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder width. Unhook and row to your belly button. The fixed track removes the need to stabilize the bar path, so you can focus on the pulling motion. Twist to re-rack at any point if you need to stop.
exercises.notes: Good entry point for learning the barbell row pattern with added safety. The fixed bar path means you can't cheat by swinging the bar outward, which teaches a clean vertical pull to the torso. Pairs naturally with Smith machine pressing for efficient supersets.

exercises.name: Landmine Row (Single-Arm)
exercises.beginner_tips: Barbell wedged in corner or landmine attachment. Stand to one side, stagger your feet, hinge forward. Row the free end up toward your hip. The thick end of the barbell forces a wide grip which can improve grip strength. Minimal lower back stress.
exercises.notes: The arcing bar path creates a unique pulling angle that hits the back differently from standard rows. Sometimes called a Meadows row when performed standing perpendicular to the bar. Great variation to cycle in when standard rows feel stale.

exercises.name: Inverted Row (Bodyweight Row)
exercises.beginner_tips: Bar at waist height on Smith machine or rack. Lie under it, grab bar, pull chest up keeping body straight like a reverse plank. Like an upside-down push-up. Higher bar = easier, lower bar = harder. Keep your body rigid — don't let your hips sag.
exercises.notes: The pulling equivalent of a push-up. Great stepping stone toward barbell rows and pull-ups. Can also be done with gymnastics rings or TRX straps, which add instability and allow grip rotation. Progression: high bar (easy) → low bar → feet elevated → weighted vest.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Cable Row
exercises.beginner_tips: Use a single D-handle on the cable row. Pull to your hip one arm at a time. You can allow a slight torso rotation — this actually increases the range of motion and is how your back works naturally. Feel each side independently.
exercises.notes: Fixes left/right back imbalances. The slight rotation allowed at the cable station actually increases ROM and matches how the lats function (they assist with torso rotation). Constant cable tension throughout. Pairs well with single-arm lat pulldown for a complete unilateral back session.

exercises.name: Pull-Up (Overhand Grip)
exercises.beginner_tips: Palms AWAY from you (overhand/pronated), grip wider than shoulders. Hang with straight arms, then pull chin over bar by driving elbows down and back. Lower with control — don't just drop. Can't do one? Use the progression: lat pulldowns → band-assisted → negatives (jump up, lower as slowly as possible) → full pull-ups.
exercises.notes: The ultimate back exercise. Overhand grip emphasizes the lats and teres major with less bicep involvement compared to chin-ups. Research shows similar lat activation across grip types (Youdas et al., 2010), so the best grip is the one that lets you do the most quality reps. Add weight with a belt or dumbbell between feet once bodyweight becomes easy for 12+ reps.

exercises.name: Chin-Up (Underhand Grip)
exercises.beginner_tips: Palms TOWARD you (underhand/supinated), about shoulder-width apart. Pull chin over bar. Generally easier than pull-ups because biceps contribute more force in this grip position.
exercises.notes: Equally effective as pull-ups for lat development — EMG research shows no significant difference in lat activation between grips. The higher bicep involvement makes these a great two-for-one exercise on pull days. Usually the first bodyweight vertical pull people master due to the mechanical advantage.

exercises.name: Neutral-Grip Pull-Up
exercises.beginner_tips: Palms face EACH OTHER (neutral grip) on parallel handles. Pull chin over the bar. This grip sits halfway between a pull-up and chin-up in difficulty and is the most comfortable for the vast majority of people's shoulders, elbows, and wrists.
exercises.notes: The most joint-friendly pull-up variation. The neutral grip places the shoulder in a packed, stable position and eliminates the wrist/forearm rotation stress of pronated and supinated grips. If pull-ups or chin-ups bother your shoulders or elbows, try these first. Requires a bar with parallel handles (common on most pull-up stations).

exercises.name: Assisted Pull-Up Machine
exercises.beginner_tips: Kneel on the pad (or stand on the platform depending on the machine). The machine pushes you up — MORE weight on the stack = MORE help (opposite of most machines). Set it to the weight that lets you do 8-12 clean reps. Gradually reduce the assistance weight each week.
exercises.notes: The bridge between lat pulldowns and real pull-ups. Track your assistance weight and reduce it by 5-10 lbs every week or two. Once you can do the movement with less than ~25% of your bodyweight in assistance, try unassisted negatives.

exercises.name: Band-Assisted Pull-Up
exercises.beginner_tips: Loop a resistance band over the pull-up bar and place one foot or knee in the loop. The band stretches and helps push you up — most at the bottom where you're weakest. Use a thicker band for more help, thinner for less. Use overhand, underhand, or neutral grip.
exercises.notes: Differs from the machine in an important way: the band provides the most assistance at the bottom (where the band is most stretched and you're weakest) and the least at the top (where the band is slack and you're strongest). This resistance profile more closely matches the actual pull-up than a machine does. Progress by using thinner bands until you don't need one.

exercises.name: Lat Pulldown
exercises.beginner_tips: Sit down, grab wide bar, lean back slightly (~15 degrees — not way back), pull to upper chest. Squeeze shoulder blades down and together. Don't pull behind neck — that's unnecessary shoulder stress with no benefit. Think about pulling your elbows INTO your back pockets.
exercises.notes: Machine version of the pull-up. Adjustable weight makes it accessible for any level. Different grips shift emphasis slightly: wide overhand increases the stretch on the lats, close neutral increases ROM and bicep involvement, underhand mimics a chin-up. All grips produce similar lat activation — variation keeps things fresh and hits slightly different angles.

exercises.name: Close-Grip Lat Pulldown (Neutral Grip)
exercises.beginner_tips: Use a V-handle or close-grip parallel attachment. Pull to upper chest, lean back slightly. This version emphasizes the lower lats and biceps more than the wide-grip version due to the greater range of motion and supination-friendly position.
exercises.notes: The neutral grip is easiest on the shoulders and wrists. The longer range of motion compared to wide-grip may actually be superior for lat growth. Good default pulldown variation for anyone with shoulder or wrist discomfort.

exercises.name: Reverse-Grip Lat Pulldown (Supinated)
exercises.beginner_tips: Same as a regular pulldown but grip the bar with palms facing TOWARD you (underhand), about shoulder-width apart. Pull to upper chest. This is essentially a chin-up on a cable machine — you'll feel your biceps working more than with an overhand grip.
exercises.notes: The cable chin-up. Great for people who can't do bodyweight chin-ups yet but want the same grip and muscle emphasis. The supinated grip puts the biceps in a strong position, so most people can pull more weight than with an overhand grip. Counts as both back and bicep work — useful on pull days when time is limited.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Lat Pulldown
exercises.beginner_tips: Use a D-handle on the lat pulldown or a high cable. Pull down to your shoulder one arm at a time. You can angle your body slightly toward the working side for a deeper lat stretch. Focus on feeling one lat contract fully before switching sides.
exercises.notes: The best way to identify and fix left/right lat imbalances — most people have a dominant side that takes over during bilateral pulldowns. The single-arm position allows a slight rotation that increases the range of motion. Pairs naturally with single-arm cable rows for a complete unilateral back session.

exercises.name: Straight-Arm Lat Pulldown
exercises.beginner_tips: Stand facing a high cable with a straight bar or rope. Arms straight with a slight fixed bend in the elbows. Push the bar down toward your thighs by squeezing your lats — your arms should stay mostly straight throughout. You should feel the contraction deep in the sides of your back.
exercises.notes: Isolates the lats without any bicep involvement — the biceps can't contribute because the elbow doesn't bend. Excellent for building the mind-muscle connection with your lats, which many beginners struggle with. Great as a lat activation exercise before pull-ups or pulldowns, or as a finisher after heavy rowing.

exercises.name: Pullover Machine (Nautilus-Style)
exercises.beginner_tips: Sit in the machine, reach back to grip the bar or pad overhead. Pull the bar down in an arc toward your lap using your lats. Arms stay relatively straight throughout — the elbows press against the pads, not your hands. The deep stretch overhead is the most valuable part of the movement.
exercises.notes: Not every gym has one, but if yours does, it provides something no other exercise can: true lat isolation with a deep stretch under load through a full range of motion, all on a guided path. The machine handles the stability so you can focus entirely on the lat contraction. Originally designed by Arthur Jones for Nautilus. Having a resurgence in well-equipped gyms.

exercises.name: Seated High Cable Row (to Face)
exercises.beginner_tips: Sit at a cable station with the pulley set high (or use the lat pulldown station with a rope attachment). Pull the rope toward your upper chest and face, driving elbows wide and squeezing upper back. Combines a row with a face pull in one movement.
exercises.notes: Hits upper back and rear delts simultaneously. Great for posture since it strengthens the muscles that pull your shoulders back and down. The high cable angle targets the mid-traps and rhomboids more than a standard low cable row.
